patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/swap: fix swap cache memcg accounting

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/swap: fix swap cache memcg accounting

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes an issue in the swap cache memcg accounting by charging the folio in the memcg before adding it to the swap cache, ensuring that accounting is done at the correct level.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with charging the folio in the memcg after adding it to the swap cache and suggested that this could cause thrashing. However, agreed that the patch is clean and good enough for now.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 20, 2026 at 06:05:59AM +0800, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> The swap readahead path was recently refactored and while doing this,
> the order between the charging of the folio in the memcg and the addition
> of the folio in the swap cache was inverted.
> 
> Since the accounting of the folio is done while adding the folio to the
> swap cache and the folio is not charged in the memcg yet, the accounting
> is then done at the node level, which is wrong.
> 
> Fix this by charging the folio in the memcg before adding it to the swap cache.
> 
> Fixes: 2732acda82c9 ("mm, swap: use swap cache as the swap in synchronize layer")
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---
>  mm/swap_state.c | 9 ++++-----
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> index 6d0eef7470be..48aff2c917c0 100644
> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> @@ -494,6 +494,10 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_prepare_and_add(swp_entry_t entry,
>  
>  	__folio_set_locked(folio);
>  	__folio_set_swapbacked(folio);
> +
> +	if (!charged && mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, NULL, gfp, entry))
> +		goto failed;
> +

Thanks! I was worrying charging it first could cause thrashing, raced
swapin will cause multiple charge and failed one will uncharge, so
I put the charge after insert into cache, completely forgot about
the lruvec static info part.

For a quick fix this this is clean and good enough, and it
doesn't effect the most performance sensitive part, mTHP and
ZRAM would have `charged == true` here so they are not effected.

Later we can move the lruvec static info out of
__swap_cache_add_folio after the charge, maybe I'll do this with
someother follow up optimzation together, then everything should
be perfect.

And I think we need Cc stable here.

Acked-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] md/raid0: use kvzalloc/kvfree for strip_zone and devlist allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] md/raid0: use kvzalloc/kvfree for strip_zone and devlist allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a memory allocation issue in the RAID0 driver by converting kzalloc/kzalloc_objs to kvzalloc/kvfree for strip_zone and devlist allocations, which helps prevent WARN_ON_ONCE_GFP() errors when raid_disks is large. The change allows for contiguous allocation with __GFP_NOWARN and falls back to vmalloc for large sizes.

review_comments.author: Yu Kuai
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag. The change is correct and should prevent the WARN_ON_ONCE_GFP() error.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/3/9 7:42, Gregory Price 写道:

> syzbot reported a WARNING at mm/page_alloc.c:__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof()
> triggered by create_strip_zones() in the RAID0 driver.
>
> When raid_disks is large, the allocation size exceeds MAX_PAGE_ORDER (4MB
> on x86), causing WARN_ON_ONCE_GFP(order > MAX_PAGE_ORDER).
>
> Convert the strip_zone and devlist allocations from kzalloc/kzalloc_objs to
> kvzalloc/kvzalloc_objs, which first attempts a contiguous allocation with
> __GFP_NOWARN and then falls back to vmalloc for large sizes. Convert the
> corresponding kfree calls to kvfree.
>
> Both arrays are pure metadata lookup tables (arrays of pointers and zone
> descriptors) accessed only via indexing, so they do not require physically
> contiguous memory.
>
> Reported-by:syzbot+924649752adf0d3ac9dd@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
> Signed-off-by: Gregory Price<gourry@gourry.net>
> ---
>   drivers/md/raid0.c | 18 +++++++++---------
>   1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 9 deletions(-)

Reviewed-by: Yu Kuai <yukuai@fnnas.com>

-- 
Thansk,
Kuai

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4] firmware_loader: add search= module option for multi-path firmware lookup

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4] firmware_loader: add search= module option for multi-path firmware lookup

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new module option called 'search=' to the firmware loader, which allows users to specify multiple paths for firmware lookup. The search path is tried after the original path and before hardcoded default paths. The search path can be specified as a colon-separated list of directories, with backslashes used as escape characters to embed literal colons or backslashes in the path.

review_comments.author: Greg Kroah-Hartman
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Greg Kroah-Hartman expressed concern about the naming of the new module option, suggesting that it should be either 'search_path' or 'path_search', as he finds the current name unclear.

Reviewer suggested moving firmware path parsing to startup or when an option is written, reducing the need for repeated cleanup logic in search paths
review_comments.raw_body:
"search_path"?

Or "path_search"?

Naming is hard :(

---

Why don't we just do all the "parsing" and stripping at startup (and
when a new option is written), so we don't have to do it each and every
time we search for a firmware image?  That would put all of the
"cleanup" logic in one place and reduce the churn a bit.

Yes, it would require another local variable, but that should be fine.

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agreed to address a concern about the firmware_class.search= module option's interaction with the original path= string, promising to make changes in a future version.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, I'll see what I can do.

Thanks,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 14/14] nfsd: add NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/14] nfsd/sunrpc: add support for netlink upcalls for mountd/exportd
series_items.patch_summary: This patchset adds support for netlink upcalls to the mountd/exportd services, replacing the outdated /proc-based interfaces used by these services. The new netlink-based interfaces provide a more modern and flexible way to handle cache notifications between the kernel and userland. This change is necessary due to limitations in the current implementation, including difficulty extending it to support new export options and reliance on /proc which may not be desirable in container environments. The patchset includes 14 patches that modify various kernel components, including nfsd, sunrpc, and netlink, to implement the new interfaces.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the unused 'struct nfsd_genl_rqstp' definition in nfsctl.c, explaining that it's used elsewhere and providing updated code to reflect its usage.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the naming consistency of upcall functions by renaming sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall(), and updating all calls to it in various files.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the function name sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout() was misleading, as it didn't actually handle timeouts. The author agreed to rename it to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn(), which warns if there are no listeners.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern that the netlink upcalls would not handle both /proc and netlink interfaces, but explained that the new cache_do_upcall() function will handle both cases.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the missing cache_notify operation in struct cache_detail, agreeing to add it in a later patch and providing the necessary code changes.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about exposing the internal cache_request structure by adding two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), which allow callers to count and snapshot pending upcall requests without exposing the struct cache_request. The author confirmed that these functions will be used by the nfsd generic netlink dumpit handler for svc_export upcall requests.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches, being part of the sunrpc module, cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family. The author created a new 'sunrpc' generic netlink family with its own multicast group to support cache upcall notifications for these caches.
review_comments.raw_body:
The auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches live in the sunrpc module,
so they cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family. Create a new
"sunrpc" generic netlink family with its own "exportd" multicast
group to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident
caches.

Define a YAML spec (sunrpc_cache.yaml) with a cache-type enum
(ip_map, unix_gid), a cache-notify multicast event, and the
corresponding uapi header.

Implement sunrpc_cache_notify() which mirrors the nfsd_cache_notify()
pattern: check for listeners on the exportd multicast group, build
and send SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY with the cache-type attribute.

Register/unregister the sunrpc_nl_family in init_sunrpc() and
cleanup_sunrpc().

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml | 40 ++++++++++++++++
 fs/nfsd/netlink.c                             |  2 +-
 include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h           | 35 ++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/Makefile                           |  2 +-
 net/sunrpc/netlink.c                          | 66 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/netlink.h                          | 25 ++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/sunrpc_syms.c                      | 10 ++++
 7 files changed, 178 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f4aa699598bca9ce0215bbc418d9ddcae25c0110
--- /dev/null
+++ b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
@@ -0,0 +1,40 @@
+# SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause)
+---
+name: sunrpc
+protocol: genetlink
+uapi-header: linux/sunrpc_netlink.h
+
+doc: SUNRPC cache upcall support over generic netlink.
+
+definitions:
+  -
+    type: flags
+    name: cache-type
+    entries: [ip_map, unix_gid]
+
+attribute-sets:
+  -
+    name: cache-notify
+    attributes:
+      -
+        name: cache-type
+        type: u32
+        enum: cache-type
+
+operations:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: cache-notify
+      doc: Notification that there are cache requests that need servicing
+      attribute-set: cache-notify
+      mcgrp: exportd
+      event:
+        attributes:
+          - cache-type
+
+mcast-groups:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: none
+    -
+      name: exportd

+

+	SUNRPC_CMD_MAX = (__SUNRPC_CMD_MAX - 1)

new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..e59ee82dfab358fc367045d9f04c394000c812ec
--- /dev/null
+++ b/net/sunrpc/netlink.c
@@ -0,0 +1,66 @@
+// SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause)
+/* Do not edit directly, auto-generated from: */
+/*	Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml */
+/* YNL-GEN kernel source */
+/* To regenerate run: tools/net/ynl/ynl-regen.sh */
+
+#include <net/netlink.h>
+#include <net/genetlink.h>
+#include <linux/sunrpc/cache.h>
+
+#include "netlink.h"
+
+#include <uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h>
+
+/* Ops table for sunrpc */
+static const struct genl_split_ops sunrpc_nl_ops[] = {
+};
+
+static const struct genl_multicast_group sunrpc_nl_mcgrps[] = {
+	[SUNRPC_NLGRP_NONE] = { "none", },
+	[SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD] = { "exportd", },
+};
+
+struct genl_family sunrpc_nl_family __ro_after_init = {
+	.name		= SUNRPC_FAMILY_NAME,
+	.version	= SUNRPC_FAMILY_VERSION,
+	.netnsok	= true,
+	.parallel_ops	= true,
+	.module		= THIS_MODULE,
+	.split_ops	= sunrpc_nl_ops,
+	.n_split_ops	= ARRAY_SIZE(sunrpc_nl_ops),
+	.mcgrps		= sunrpc_nl_mcgrps,
+	.n_mcgrps	= ARRAY_SIZE(sunrpc_nl_mcgrps),
+};
+
+int sunrpc_cache_notify(struct cache_detail *cd, struct cache_head *h,
+			u32 cache_type)
+{
+	struct genlmsghdr *hdr;
+	struct sk_buff *msg;
+
+	if (!genl_has_listeners(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net,
+				SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD))
+		return -ENOLINK;
+
+	msg = genlmsg_new(GENLMSG_DEFAULT_SIZE, GFP_KERNEL);
+	if (!msg)
+		return -ENOMEM;
+
+	hdr = genlmsg_put(msg, 0, 0, &sunrpc_nl_family, 0,
+			  SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY);
+	if (!hdr) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	if (nla_put_u32(msg, SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE, cache_type)) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	genlmsg_end(msg, hdr);
+	return genlmsg_multicast_netns(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net, msg, 0,
+				       SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD, GFP_KERNEL);
+}
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(sunrpc_cache_notify);

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever suggested that adding the new netlink upcall for mountd/exportd might be premature and should be moved to a subsequent patch in the series.

Reviewer noted that when a thread returns -EAGAIN from cache_do_upcall(), the CACHE_PENDING bit is already cleared by another thread, causing a netlink notification for an entry that is no longer pending, and suggested dropping the spinlock before calling wake_up() to avoid this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Nit: Adding this here might be premature. Should it be moved to a
subsequent patch in this series?

---

When ret == -EAGAIN, the CACHE_PENDING bit was already cleared by
another thread that won the race. Calling cache_notify in this case
sends a netlink notification for an entry that is no longer pending. The
winning thread already queued the request and will trigger its own
notification. If you agree this observation is not a false positive, a
possible fix might be:

  spin_unlock(&detail->queue_lock);
  if (ret != -EAGAIN && detail->cache_notify)
      detail->cache_notify(detail, h);
  wake_up(&detail->queue_wait);


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the sunrpc_cache_requests_count() function returns a count of pending cache requests, but this count can become stale as soon as the queue_lock is dropped, leading to incomplete data being returned to userspace in one-shot dumpit handlers.
review_comments.raw_body:
This API architecture introduces a TOCTOU, since as soon as the
queue_lock is dropped, the count can immediately become stale.

The count returned by sunrpc_cache_requests_count() is used as the array
bound. To wit:

  cnt = sunrpc_cache_requests_count(cd);

  items = kcalloc(cnt, sizeof(*items), GFP_KERNEL);

  seqnos = kcalloc(cnt, sizeof(*seqnos), GFP_KERNEL);

  cnt = sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(cd, items, seqnos, cnt);



This appears in all four dumpit handlers (ip_map, unix_gid, svc_export,
expkey).

This isn't a memory safety issue; _snapshot() caps its output at max, so
if the list grows between the two calls, entries are silently truncated
rather than overflowing the buffer. If the list shrinks, the arrays are
merely oversized.

However, the practical risk is incomplete data returned to userspace. If
the caller is guaranteed to be re-invoked (e.g., the netlink dumpit
callback gets called again until it returns 0), then missing items due
to list growth between count() and snapshot() is harmless: they'll be
picked up on the next pass.

But looking at the callers, they all do this:

  if (cb->args[0])
      return 0;

and then set cb->args[0] after the single snapshot dump.

The dumpit is a one-shot: it snapshots once and signals completion. If
the list grows between count() and snapshot(), the truncated entries are
silently lost and there's no subsequent pass to pick them up, IIUC.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Chuck Lever's concern about a potential race condition between the kernel and userland, explaining that userland will receive separate notifications for each queued cache request, ensuring it can pick up any remaining entries on subsequent passes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Userland will receive a separate notify request whenever a
cache_request is queued, and that notification is only sent after the
cache_request is queued.

So while it might not receive all of the queued requests from the
kernel in the race you describe, it won't matter because select() will
return the notify descriptor again on the next pass and mountd will
pick up the remaining entries at that point.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that sunrpc_nl_unix_gid_get_reqs_dumpit() can overflow the SKB buffer due to packing too many reply entries into a single netlink message, leading to indefinite retries by the netlink subsystem when nla_put fails. He suggested implementing proper netlink dump continuation using a cursor in cb->args.
review_comments.raw_body:
sunrpc_nl_unix_gid_get_reqs_dumpit() packs its reply entries into a
single netlink message. The default SKB size is ~8 KiB, and each entry
encodes to ~24 bytes, so the buffer fills at roughly 340 entries. When
nla_put fails, the function returns -ENOBUFS but never sets

  cb->args[0] = 1;

causing the netlink subsystem to retry indefinitely. A big NFS server
can easily exceed 340 pending GID cache entries.

Should we implement proper netlink dump continuation: snapshot once
then emit entries across multiple dumpit calls using a cursor in
cb->args?

-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that several attributes and commands in the patch have no usage, suggesting they be removed or deferred to a later patch where they are used; also recommended updating NLA policy masks to use named constants for better maintainability.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is the last patch in this series that applied cleanly to the
current nfsd-testing branch, so I'm stopping with this one.

I'm going to whine only a little about the commit message being not even
a full sentence. </whine>

review-prompts seems to think that removing sunrpc_cache_notify in this
patch will break the build (temporarily). I mention that only for due
diligence -- my earlier request to move sunrpc_cache_notify out of
tool-generated files will probably make this moot.

The svc-export-req attribute set isn't used in this patch; perhaps
its introduction should be deferred to a patch where it is used.
NFSD_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY looks like the same: dead code for now.

0x3ffff and 0x7 in the NLA policy masks correspond to NFSEXP_ALLFLAGS
and NFSEXP_XPRTSEC_ALL from include/uapi/linux/nfsd/export.h, but the
policy uses raw hex. Adding a new export flag requires updating three
independent locations with no compile-time check. Using the named
constants directly in the policy would make the link more explicit. I
don't have a good suggestion about the list of flags in the YAML spec.

Several AI reviewers noted that GENLMSG_DEFAULT_SIZE is 8KB, yet the
request and reply attributes for some of the commands added in this
series are no larger than sizeof(u32).

Recommend you add Jakub to the cc: for the series for closer human
inspection of the YAML / netlink protocol aspects.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/14] sunrpc: add helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), to the Linux kernel's sunrpc module. These functions allow callers to count and snapshot the pending upcall request list without exposing internal data structures. The count function returns the number of requests with CACHE_PENDING set, while the snapshot function takes a reference on each item so it can be safely used after the queue lock is released. This patch is part of a larger series and will be used by the nfsd generic netlink dumpit handler for svc_export upcall requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/14] sunrpc: add a generic netlink family for cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic netlink family called 'sunrpc' to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident caches. The new family is defined in a YAML spec and includes a multicast group called 'exportd'. A function, `sunrpc_cache_notify()`, is implemented to mirror the pattern of `nfsd_cache_notify()` and sends SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY with the cache-type attribute. The patch also registers and unregisters the sunrpc_nl_family in init_sunrpc() and cleanup_sunrpc(). This change allows for cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident caches, which were previously not supported.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the ip_map (auth.unix.ip) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. constants directly in the policy would make the link more explicit. I
don't have a good suggestion about the list of flags in the YAML spec.

Several AI reviewers noted that GENLMSG_DEFAULT_SIZE is 8KB, yet the
request and reply attributes for some of the commands added in this
series are no larger than sizeof(u32).

Recommend you add Jakub to the cc: for the series for closer human
inspection of the YAML / netlink protocol aspects.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the svc_export (nfsd.export) cache in the Linux kernel's NFS server component. It introduces a new callback function, nfsd_cache_notify(), which sends a multicast event to userspace when there are pending requests in the cache. The patch also implements two new functions: nfsd_nl_svc_export_get_reqs_dumpit() and nfsd_nl_svc_export_set_reqs_doit(). The first function dumps pending svc_export cache requests over netlink, while the second function parses responses from userspace and updates the cache accordingly. This allows for more efficient management of the svc_export cache and improves communication between the kernel and userspace.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the NFS server's expkey (nfsd.fh) cache, allowing clients to notify the server of changes to cached file handles. The patch introduces a new attribute set and operations for expkey cache management, including dumping pending requests and updating the cache with client-provided information. It also wires up the expkey_notify callback to trigger multicast events when cache misses occur. This change follows the pattern established by existing svc_export netlink support and is intended to improve the efficiency of NFS server caching.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/14] sunrpc: add SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command that allows userspace to flush the sunrpc auth caches (ip_map and unix_gid) without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, providing a fallback to /proc for older kernels. An optional attribute selects which caches to flush, or all caches if omitted.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new netlink command, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH, which allows userspace to flush the nfsd export caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, NFSD_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, that selects which caches to flush. If omitted, all nfsd caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, with /proc fallback for older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/1] writeback: don't block sync for filesystems with no data integrity guarantees
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new superblock flag, SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY, to indicate filesystems that cannot guarantee data persistence on sync operations. For such filesystems, the sync operation will kick off writeback of dirty inodes but will not wait for the flusher threads to complete the writeback. This change is necessary because waiting on the flusher threads can cause hangs, as seen in a recent report for fuse-overlayfs. The patch replaces an earlier per-inode flag and allows skipping unnecessary iteration over dirty inodes during sync operations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/8] io_uring: add kernel-managed buffer rings

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/8] io_uring: add kernel-managed buffer rings

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/8] io_uring/kbuf: add support for kernel-managed buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for kernel-managed buffer rings to the io_uring feature. It allows the kernel to allocate and manage the backing buffers for a buffer ring, eliminating the need for applications to provide and manage them. The patch introduces new flags and structures to enable this functionality, including IOU_PBUF_RING_KERNEL_MANAGED and the buf_size field in the io_uring_buf_reg struct. When an application creates a kernel-managed buffer ring, it must set these flags and use mmap(2) to obtain a virtual mapping of the buffers. This approach simplifies memory management for applications using io_uring and improves performance by reducing overhead associated with user-space buffer allocation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/8] io_uring/kbuf: support kernel-managed buffer rings in buffer selection
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for kernel-managed buffer rings in the io_uring/kbuf module. It modifies the io_br_sel struct to separate address and value fields, allowing for error checking on kernel addresses. The patch also introduces auto-committing of selected kernel-managed buffers. This change enables the use of kernel-managed buffers with io_uring, improving performance and efficiency.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/8] io_uring/kbuf: add buffer ring pinning/unpinning
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds kernel APIs to pin and unpin buffer rings, preventing userspace from unregistering a pinned ring. This is necessary for fuse to safely access buffer ring contents in atomic contexts. The patch introduces two new functions: io_uring_buf_ring_pin and io_uring_buf_ring_unpin, which allow the kernel to pin and unpin buffer rings respectively. When a buffer ring is pinned, userspace cannot unregister it until it is explicitly unpinned, preventing potential data corruption or crashes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/8] io_uring/kbuf: return buffer id in buffer selection
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the io_uring/kbuf module to return the ID of the selected buffer in addition to its address and size when selecting a buffer for multishot I/O operations. This is necessary for kernel-managed buffer rings, which require the ability to recycle previously used buffers. The changes affect three files: include/linux/io_uring/cmd.h, include/linux/io_uring_types.h, and io_uring/kbuf.c. The modifications involve adding a new field to the struct io_br_sel data structure to hold the buffer ID and updating the io_ring_buffer_select and io_buffer_select functions to populate this field.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/8] io_uring/kbuf: add recycling for kernel managed buffer rings
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an interface to recycle kernel-managed buffers back into a buffer ring, allowing for more efficient use of memory and reducing the need for frequent allocations. The recycling process involves checking if the buffer can be safely reused, updating its metadata, and incrementing the tail pointer in the buffer ring. This feature is designed to improve performance by minimizing memory fragmentation and reducing the overhead of buffer allocation and deallocation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/8] io_uring/kbuf: add io_uring_is_kmbuf_ring()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a function io_uring_is_kmbuf_ring() to the Linux kernel's io_uring module, which checks if a buffer ring is managed by the kernel at a specified buffer group. The function returns true if it finds such a ring and false otherwise. This preparatory patch is part of an upcoming fuse kernel-managed buffer support, where the server needs to ensure that the registered buffer ring is a kernel-managed one. The function is implemented in io_uring/kbuf.c and exported as a symbol for use by other parts of the kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/8] io_uring/kbuf: export io_ring_buffer_select()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the io_ring_buffer_select() function to allow it to be used by callers who pass in a pinned buffer without needing to grab the io_uring mutex. This is necessary for fuse io-uring, which needs to select a buffer from a kernel-managed buffer ring while the uring mutex may already be held and may need to select a buffer in atomic contexts.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 8/8] io_uring/cmd: set selected buffer index in __io_uring_cmd_done()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch sets the selected buffer index in the completion queue entry for io_uring commands that select a buffer, allowing userspace to determine which buffer contains the data. The change is necessary to provide this information to userspace and was implemented by adding a new flag IORING_CQE_F_BUFFER and encoding the buffer index in the completion queue entry.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces kernel-managed buffer rings for io_uring, where the kernel allocates and manages buffers on behalf of the application. This eliminates the need for applications to allocate and manage backing buffers. The design was influenced by a discussion regarding alternative approaches, including allocating buffers through memory regions or registered buffers interface. The patches modify various io_uring files to support kernel-managed buffer rings, including adding support for pinning and unpinning buffers, recycling buffers, and exporting functions related to buffer selection.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
reviewer requested a design discussion on the fuse implementation in io_uring

Reviewer noted that the introduction of kernel-managed buffer rings (kbuf) makes it difficult to support different request sizes, as each size would require a separate kbuf size, which is not ideal for non-privileged users and leads to duplicated effort. Bernd suggested using pbuf for non-privileged users and deprecating the existing fuse io-uring buffer API.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

I'm a bit late, but could we have a design discussion about fuse here?

---

request sizes for the ring buffers. Without kbuf I thought we would just
register entries with different sizes, which would then get sorted into
per size lists. Now with kbuf that will not work anymore and we need
different kbuf sizes. But then kbuf is not suitable for non-privileged
users. So in order to support different request sizes one basically has
to implement things two times - not ideal. Couldn't we have pbuf for
non-privileged users and basically depcrecate the existing fuse io-uring
buffer API? In the sense that it needs to be further supported for some
time, but won't get any new feature. Different buffer sizes would then
only be supported through kbuf/pbuf?


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author Joanne Koong is addressing Bernd's feedback on adding an 'if (bl)' check for recycling API, and she confirms that reducing memory footprint of buffers was the main reason for choosing the buffer ring design.

The author is addressing Bernd's feedback on adding an 'if (bl)' check for recycling API in the kernel-managed buffer rings patch. The author explains that variable-sized buffers would lead to inefficient allocation and tracking, and instead advocates for incremental buffer consumption.

The author addressed Bernd's concern that the patch did not handle the case of privileged fuse servers, explaining that it's only zero-copying that's not possible for non-privileged servers.

Author addressed a concern about using pbufs for kernel-managed buffer rings, explaining that kbufs are sufficient and can be used by both non-privileged and privileged servers, and that the kernel needs to recycle buffers in this case.

Author acknowledged that using registered memory regions instead of registered buffers would avoid per I/O lookup overhead and agreed to update the fuse patches to use this approach, which will also eliminate the need for a refcount dance.
review_comments.raw_body:
Is your motivation for wanting different request sizes for the ring
buffers so that it can optimize the memory costs of the buffers? I
agree that trying to reduce the memory footprint of the buffers is
very important. The main reason I ended up going with the buffer ring
design was for that purpose. When kbuf incremental buffer consumption
is added in the future (I plan to submit it separately once all the
io-uring pieces of the fuse-zero-copy patchset land), this will allow
non-overlapping regions of the individual buffer to be used across
multiple different-sized requests concurrently.

---

buffers upfront because:
a) entries are fully maximized. With variable-sized buffers, the big
buffers would be reserved specifically for payload requests while the
small buffers would be reserved specifically for metadata requests. We
could allocate '# entries' amount of small buffers, but for big
buffers there would be less than '# entries'. If the server needs to
service a lot of concurrent I/O requests, then the ring gets throttled
on the limited number of big buffers available.

b) it best maximizes buffer memory. A request could need a buffer of
any size so with variable-sized buffers, there's extra space in the
buffer that is still being wasted. For example, for large payload
requests, the big buffers would need to be the size of the max payload
size (eg default 1 MB) but a lot of requests will fall under that.
With incremental buffer consumption, only however many bytes used by
the request are reserved in the buffer.

c) there's no overhead with having to (as you pointed out) keep the
buffers tracked and sorted into per-sized lists. If we wanted to use
variable-sized buffers with kbufs instead of using incremental buffer
consumption, the best way to do that would be to allocate a separate
kbufring to support payload requests vs metadata requests.

---

Non-privileged fuse servers use kbufs as well. It's only zero-copying
that is not possible for non-privileged servers.

---

I don't think this is necessary because kbufs works for both
non-privileged and privileged servers. For how the buffer gets used by
the server/kernel, pbufs are not an option here because the kernel has
to be the one to recycle back the buffer (since it needs to read /
copy data the server returns back in the buffer).

---

I hope I understood your questions correctly, but if I misread
anything, please let me know. I am going to be updating and submitting
the fuse patches next week - the main update will be changing the
headers to go through a registered memory region (which I only
realized existed after the discussion with Pavel in v1) instead of as
a registered buffer, as that will allow us to avoid the per I/O lookup
overhead and drop the patch for the
"io_uring_fixed_index_get()/io_uring_fixed_index_put()" refcount dance
altogether.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Bernd Schubert noted that non-privileged processes cannot pin buffers by default due to a minimum mlock size of 8MB and questioned whether kbufs are always pinned, requesting a re-read of the code.

The reviewer suggested setting a flag or taking a reference count on the pbuf to prevent its destruction, allowing kernel-managed buffer rings to be reused.

The reviewer, Bernd Schubert, did not provide any specific technical comments or concerns about the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Non-privileged cannot pin, at least by default mlock size is 8MB. I was
under the impression that kbuf would be always pinned, but I need to
read over it again.

---

I was thinking to set a flag or take ref count and to disallow pbuf
destruction.

---

I will try to review ASAP when you submit.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that I/O heavy workloads may be bottlenecked by the limited number of large buffers, but did not indicate a plan to address this issue.

The author addressed concerns about potential fragmentation and slow request handling in kernel-managed buffer rings by explaining that buffers are recycled as a whole once all requests have completed, and proposing mitigation strategies such as assigning requests to the nearest numa node or using variable-sized buffers.

The author addressed Bernd's concern about the 'if (bl)' check in the recycling API, explaining that the kernel-managed buffer rings are accounted to the user's mlock usage and will be limited by their group's mlock limits.

Author acknowledged a concern about preventing buffer freeing in userspace, explained that pinning buffers is an alternative solution, and noted that it would require attributing buffers to the mlock count.

Author acknowledged that the 'if (bl)' check is a necessary fix and agreed to include it in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
My worry is that for I/O heavy workloads with large read/write
payloads (eg client access patterns reading/writing MBs at a time),
the limited number of big enough buffers becomes the throttling
bottleneck.

---

With incremetnal buffer consumption, there's not fragmentation in the
classical sense (eg scattered unusable holes). The buffer gets
recycled back into the ring as a whole once all the requests in it
have completed (tracked by refcounting).

I think the concern is that if the server is very slow to fulfill
requests and the workload pattern has it so that slow requests are
packed into the same buffer as fast requests across all the buffers in
the queue and that queue has all its buffers saturated, then the next
buffer is available only once the slow request has completed. We can
mitigate this by assigning the request to a queue on the nearest numa
node as a fallback if we detect that case. We could also do the same
thing to mitigate the variable-sized buffer scenario where there's not
enough big buffers for the queue, but I think that logic ends up a bit
more complex.

I think overall we're able to support both incremental buffer
consumption + variable-sized buffers if there's a need for it in the
future where the server would like to choose.

---

The kbufs get accounted to the user's mlock usage (this happens in
__io_account_mem()). If the user running the unprivileged server
doesn't belong to a group that has high enough mlock limits, they'll
have to use regular fuse over-io-uring buffers instead of kbufs for
most of their queues.

---

I don't think we can prevent the buffers from being freed by userspace
except by pinning them but then that would need to attribute the
buffers towards the mlock count.

---

Thank you!

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: The reviewer pointed out that unprivileged servers will not be able to use kernel-managed buffer rings (kbufs) due to their inability to pin buffers, and suggested allowing unpinned pbufs instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
That is exactly what I mean - in reality  unprivileged servers will not
be able to use kbufs. And there it would be good, if that server could
use unpinned pbufs.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Joanne Koong strongly disagrees with using pbufs for kernel-managed buffer rings, citing issues with userspace modifying the buffers and refcount tracking. She suggests alternative solutions such as variable-sized buffers or reducing queue entries for unprivileged servers.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think it's fundamentally wrong to use pbufs here. The buffers are
completely under the sovereignty of userspace. If userspace chooses to
free the buffers at any point or reuse the buffer for other purposes
(or a free() and later malloc() ends up mapping to the same adddres),
the kernel should not still be accessing the data in that buffer. When
it's userspace that recycles back the buffer, this isn't a problem
because userspace maintains direct control of the lifecycle and kernel
usage of the buffer (eg if the buffer shouldn't be used by the kernel
anymore since it's freed, then userspace simply won't add it back to
the ring). This gets worse with incremental buffer consumption where
multiple requests now simultaneously use the buffer - the server can't
know when the kernel finishes reading each sub-region and on the
kernel side, refcount tracking the pbuf becomes impractical. The pbuf
buffers are a shared mapping that userspace can dynamically modify at
any time, they can add and remove buffers from the ring at will. We
can't add the refcount to the backing buffer struct directly since
that is modifiable by userspace; we would have to track it separately
in its own kernel-private list, but keeping that list in sync is
difficult when userspace can add or remove buffers through the shared
mapping at any time invisibly to the kernel.

I agree that for unprivileged servers constrained by low mlock limits,
it's annoying that they can't use these features, but I don't think
pbufs are the right direction. I think for those unprivileged servers,
if the memory consumption is a pressing issue, then we should either
just add the variable-sized buffers you mentioned or the servers
should just use fewer queues and entries and accept the tradeoff that
running unprivileged has a lower performance ceiling.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

With incremetnal buffer consumption, there's not fragmentation in the
classical sense (eg scattered unusable holes). The buffer gets
recycled back into the ring as a whole once all the requests in it
have completed (tracked by refcounting).

I think the concern is that if the server is very slow to fulfill
requests and the workload pattern has it so that slow requests are
packed into the same buffer as fast requests across all the buffers in
the queue and that queue has all its buffers saturated, then the next
buffer is available only once the slow request has completed. We can
mitigate this by assigning the request to a queue on the nearest numa
node as a fallback if we detect that case. We could also do the same
thing to mitigate the variable-sized buffer scenario where there's not
enough big buffers for the queue, but I think that logic ends up a bit
more complex.

I think overall we're able to support both incremental buffer
consumption + variable-sized buffers if there's a need for it in the
future where the server would like to choose.

---

The kbufs get accounted to the user's mlock usage (this happens in
__io_account_mem()). If the user running the unprivileged server
doesn't belong to a group that has high enough mlock limits, they'll
have to use regular fuse over-io-uring buffers instead of kbufs for
most of their queues.

---

I don't think we can prevent the buffers from being freed by userspace
except by pinning them but then that would need to attribute the
buffers towards the mlock count.

---

Thank you!

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that unprivileged servers will not be able to use kbufs due to mlock limits and suggested using unpinned pbufs as an alternative
review_comments.raw_body:
That is exactly what I mean - in reality  unprivileged servers will not
be able to use kbufs. And there it would be good, if that server could
use unpinned pbufs.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed concerns about using pbufs for kernel-managed buffer rings, arguing that it's fundamentally wrong due to userspace control over the buffers' lifecycle and refcount tracking difficulties. They suggested alternative solutions for unprivileged servers with low mlock limits.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think it's fundamentally wrong to use pbufs here. The buffers are
completely under the sovereignty of userspace. If userspace chooses to
free the buffers at any point or reuse the buffer for other purposes
(or a free() and later malloc() ends up mapping to the same adddres),
the kernel should not still be accessing the data in that buffer. When
it's userspace that recycles back the buffer, this isn't a problem
because userspace maintains direct control of the lifecycle and kernel
usage of the buffer (eg if the buffer shouldn't be used by the kernel
anymore since it's freed, then userspace simply won't add it back to
the ring). This gets worse with incremental buffer consumption where
multiple requests now simultaneously use the buffer - the server can't
know when the kernel finishes reading each sub-region and on the
kernel side, refcount tracking the pbuf becomes impractical. The pbuf
buffers are a shared mapping that userspace can dynamically modify at
any time, they can add and remove buffers from the ring at will. We
can't add the refcount to the backing buffer struct directly since
that is modifiable by userspace; we would have to track it separately
in its own kernel-private list, but keeping that list in sync is
difficult when userspace can add or remove buffers through the shared
mapping at any time invisibly to the kernel.

I agree that for unprivileged servers constrained by low mlock limits,
it's annoying that they can't use these features, but I don't think
pbufs are the right direction. I think for those unprivileged servers,
if the memory consumption is a pressing issue, then we should either
just add the variable-sized buffers you mentioned or the servers
should just use fewer queues and entries and accept the tradeoff that
running unprivileged has a lower performance ceiling.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: prevent direct reclaim during compressed readahead
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents direct reclaim during compressed readahead in Btrfs by passing specific GFP flags when allocating memory for readahead operations. The new gfp_t API is used to restrict direct reclaim, and a bool parameter is added to the add_ra_bio_pages function to allow callers to specify whether direct reclaim should be restricted or not. This change affects the btrfs_submit_compressed_read function and the add_ra_bio_pages function in fs/btrfs/compression.c.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo requested that the patch explanation provide a clear justification for why the existing GFP flags are problematic, as stated in the cover letter.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns that the patch may cause readahead to be skipped for large folios, potentially leading to re-reads and re-decompression, and suggested disabling compressed readahead altogether and relying on large folios instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
I believe it will be more convincing to explain why the current gfp 
flags is going to cause the problem you mentioned in the cover letter.

---

After reading the code, I have a feeling that, we shouldn't act on the 
behalf of MM layer to add the next few folios into the page cache.

On the other hand, with the incoming large folios, we will completely 
skip the readahead for large folios.

I know this is not optimal as the next few folios may still belong to 
the same compressed extent and will cause re-read and re-decompression.

Thus I'm wondering, for your specific workload, will disabling 
compressed ra completely and fully rely on large folios help?

If the performance is acceptable, I'd prefer to disable compressed 
readahead completely and rely on large folios instead.

(Now I understand why other fses with compression support is completely 
relying on fixed IO size)

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch prevents direct reclaim during compressed readahead in Btrfs file systems to reduce latency and prevent CPU stalls. When a bio is marked for readahead, it uses different GFP flags that exclude __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM to avoid triggering direct reclaim. This change allows the system to handle memory pressure without causing long-running reclaim operations that can lead to CPU stalls.

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo requested a clearer explanation of why using the existing GFP flags will lead to direct reclaim during compressed readahead, as described in the patch's cover letter.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns that the patch may not be optimal, as it could lead to re-read and re-decompression of folios that belong to the same compressed extent. They suggested disabling compressed readahead completely and relying on large folios instead, which they believe is a more straightforward approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
I believe it will be more convincing to explain why the current gfp 
flags is going to cause the problem you mentioned in the cover letter.

---

After reading the code, I have a feeling that, we shouldn't act on the 
behalf of MM layer to add the next few folios into the page cache.

On the other hand, with the incoming large folios, we will completely 
skip the readahead for large folios.

I know this is not optimal as the next few folios may still belong to 
the same compressed extent and will cause re-read and re-decompression.

Thus I'm wondering, for your specific workload, will disabling 
compressed ra completely and fully rely on large folios help?

If the performance is acceptable, I'd prefer to disable compressed 
readahead completely and rely on large folios instead.

(Now I understand why other fses with compression support is completely 
relying on fixed IO size)

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: avoid GFP_ATOMIC allocations in qgroup free paths

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: avoid GFP_ATOMIC allocations in qgroup free paths

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a GFP_ATOMIC allocation issue in the btrfs qgroup free paths by introducing a sentinel value to indicate that only bytes changed should be tracked, thus avoiding unnecessary allocations.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Identified the patch as a good solution to the GFP_ATOMIC allocation issue, with no major concerns or suggestions for improvement.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/3/20 10:19, Leo Martins 写道:
> When qgroups are enabled, __btrfs_qgroup_release_data() and
> qgroup_free_reserved_data() pass an extent_changeset to
> btrfs_clear_record_extent_bits() to track how many bytes had their
> EXTENT_QGROUP_RESERVED bits cleared. Inside the extent IO tree spinlock,
> add_extent_changeset() calls ulist_add() with GFP_ATOMIC to record each
> changed range. If this allocation fails, it hits a BUG_ON and panics the
> kernel.
> 
> However, both of these callers only read changeset.bytes_changed
> afterwards — the range_changed ulist is populated and immediately freed
> without ever being iterated. The GFP_ATOMIC allocation is entirely
> unnecessary for these paths.
> 
> Introduce extent_changeset_init_bytes_only() which uses a sentinel value
> (EXTENT_CHANGESET_BYTES_ONLY) on the ulist's prealloc field to signal
> that only bytes_changed should be tracked. add_extent_changeset() checks
> for this sentinel and returns early after updating bytes_changed,
> skipping the ulist_add() call entirely. This eliminates the GFP_ATOMIC
> allocation and makes the BUG_ON unreachable for these paths.
> 
> Callers that need range tracking (qgroup_reserve_data,
> qgroup_unreserve_range, btrfs_qgroup_check_reserved_leak) continue to
> use extent_changeset_init() and are unaffected.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Leo Martins <loemra.dev@gmail.com>

Looks good to me.

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add test for BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a test for the new BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl, which retrieves file checksums from the kernel's csum tree. The test covers various scenarios including regular data extents, holes, preallocated extents, buffer continuation, and checksum consistency. It also checks querying past file data and precomputed values. A new source file `btrfs_get_csums.c` is added to provide a test helper for this ioctl, and the Makefile is updated to include it. The test itself is implemented in a new test file `343`, which exercises the BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl with various inputs and checks the output against expected results.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl (input/output control) called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without needing to read the data itself. The ioctl takes an argument specifying the offset and length of interest, as well as a buffer to store the results. The kernel returns a struct containing information about whether the checksum is available, and if so, what type it is (e.g., sparse, preallocated, or compressed). This feature is primarily intended for speeding up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir operations, particularly when using the --reflink option, which can result in significant performance improvements.

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch does not address mkfs.btrfs performance and instead pointed to a separate pull request for improvements.
review_comments.raw_body:
mkfs patches for this: https://github.com/kdave/btrfs-progs/pull/1096

On 20/03/2026 12.50 pm, Mark Harmstone wrote:

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns about the handling of hole and preallocated ranges in the BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE type, suggesting that they should be treated differently due to their distinct implications for mkfs behavior. Specifically, holes should not create any extent instead of writing zero, while preallocated ranges can indicate no csums to allow mkfs to distinguish between them and change to zero writes accordingly.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested adding a special flag to indicate when csums are skipped for compressed extents, instead of using NO_CSUMS, to improve distinguishability between holes and compressed data.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested combining btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry and real buffer for csum into one structure, and removing redundant offset/length fields from the entry structure.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not sure if it's a good idea to put hole and preallocated range into 
the same BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE.

Although both means there is no csum, hole case means there is really no 
data extent, thus we should not create any extent instead of writing zero.

For preallocated, indicating it has no CSUM can allow mkfs to 
distinguish hole and preallocated, thus change to zero writes to 
prealloc, which is faster and make the resulted fs more aligned to the 
source dir.


And for EOF checks, I think we don't need to bother that much, aka, just 
let it return the regular results.

My assumption is, the mkfs shouldn't pass a range completely beyond the 
round_up(i_size), as non-reflink rootdir population would always read 
out the content of the inode from the host fs.
Thus we won't really read beyond the inode size.

---

I agree with the skip of compressed extents, but I'd prefer to have a 
special flag to indicate that, other than NO_CSUMS.

Or mkfs is unable to distinguish hole and compressed extents.

[...]

---

From the progs usage, it is always a single btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry 
at the beginning of buf[], then real buffer for csum, can we just 
combine both structures into one?

Furthermore, since we only query one extent at one time, the 
offset/length are more or less duplicated between args and entry structure.

We can just save the length into the args without the need for entry 
members (except the type).

Thanks,
Qu

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 00/21] Virtual Swap Space
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series implements virtual swap space, a new design for swap management that dynamically allocates virtual slots for swapped-out pages and associates them with physical swap slots on the swapfile or partition. The virtual swap slot is used to index into swap data structures such as the swap cache and zswap tree, replacing the old physical swap slot. The patch series also repurposes the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map to look up virtual swap slots from their associated physical swap slots.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: No review comments have been made yet. The thread is waiting for reviewers to provide feedback.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/21] mm/swap: decouple swap cache from physical swap infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the swap cache from physical swap infrastructure, preparing for virtualization of swap space. It removes swap cache related helpers from the swap table and moves to a single global lock for synchronization. The swap cache will be re-partitioned into virtual swap clusters once it is moved to the virtual swap layer. This change is temporary and will be repurposed later as an rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping).

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/21] swap: rearrange the swap header file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the swap header file (include/linux/swap.h) to group related functions into categories such as lifecycle swap, swap cache, and physical swap allocator/swap device APIs. The goal is a cleaner organization of the code without introducing any functional changes. Extern keywords are removed from the functions that were rearranged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/21] mm: swap: add an abstract API for locking out swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an abstract API to lock out swapoff in the Linux kernel's memory management subsystem. The current implementation of locking out swapoff is specific to the backing swap device and does not work well with the new virtual swap design, where a swap entry may not have a backing swap device. To address this issue, the patch adds two new functions: `tryget_swap_entry` and `put_swap_entry`, which provide a generic way to lock out swapoff for any swap entry. The patch also updates several kernel files to use these new functions instead of the old implementation. This change prepares the kernel for the virtual swap design and improves its flexibility in handling different types of swap entries.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/21] zswap: add new helpers for zswap entry operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new helper functions to abstract away zswap entry operations, making it easier to re-implement these functions when swap is virtualized. The helpers include storing, loading, erasing, and checking if a zswap entry is empty. These functions use the xarray data structure to manage the zswap entries. The patch modifies several existing functions in the mm/zswap.c file to use these new helpers, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/21] mm/swap: add a new function to check if a swap entry is in swap cached.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called `is_swap_cached` to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) module. The function checks if a given swap entry is cached in the system's swap cache. This is done by directly accessing the swap map of the corresponding swap information structure, which minimizes overhead and ensures accurate results. The patch also updates several other functions to use this new function instead of directly checking the swap map. The motivation behind this change is to abstract away the check for swap cached state, as it will soon be managed at the virtual swap layer.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/21] mm: swap: add a separate type for physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new type, swp_slot_t, to represent physical swap slots on swap devices. It separates the logical view of swap entries from their physical backing state, allowing for future virtualization and dynamic allocation of swap slots. The mapping between the two types is currently an identity mapping, but will be used in later patches to associate swapped-out pages with a backing store.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/21] mm: create scaffolds for the new virtual swap implementation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds scaffolding code for the new virtual swap implementation, including a new mm/vswap.c source file and necessary changes to the Makefile and other related files. The vswap debugfs directory is set up, but no behavioral changes are intended at this time. Additionally, Johannes Weiner is added as a reviewer for the virtual swap development.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/21] zswap: prepare zswap for swap virtualization
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the zswap code for swap virtualization by removing the range partition logic and replacing it with a simple unified zswap tree. The changes include updating the zswap.h header file to remove unused functions, modifying the swapfile.c file to remove calls to these functions, and updating the zswap.c file to use the new unified zswap tree. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to virtualize swap space in Linux.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/21] mm: swap: allocate a virtual swap slot for each swapped out page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new virtual swap slot design, where each swapped out page is dynamically allocated a virtual slot and associated metadata structure. The virtual slot replaces the physical swap slot in page table entries and is used to index into swap data structures. The patch also repurposes the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map to look up the virtual swap slot from its associated physical swap slot on the swapfile. This change improves the management of swapped out pages and enables future optimizations such as simplified swapoff.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/21] swap: move swap cache to virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the swap cache and workingset shadow for anonymous pages to the virtual swap descriptor, effectively range-partitioning the swap cache by virtual swap clusters of PMD size. This change eliminates lock contention in the swap cache, improving performance. The patch updates several kernel files, including huge_memory.c, migrate.c, shmem.c, and vmscan.c, to use the new virtual swap descriptor instead of the global swap cache lock. The approach taken is to introduce a per-folio swap pointer that allows for more efficient locking and reduces contention in the swap cache.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/21] zswap: move zswap entry management to the virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves zswap entry management from the zswap tree to the virtual swap descriptor, eliminating lock contention between zswap entries. The change re-partitions the zswap pool by virtual swap cluster and introduces new functions for storing, loading, and erasing zswap entries in the swap descriptor. This approach simplifies the management of zswap entries and improves performance by reducing lock contention.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/21] swap: implement the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the swap_cgroup API to use virtual swap, allowing for dynamic allocation of memory for swap entries' cgroup information. This reduces memory overhead in large but sparsely used swap spaces by only allocating memory when needed. The change involves moving the swap entry's cgroup information from a static array to the swap descriptor and updating various functions that interact with the swap_cgroup API.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/21] swap: manage swap entry lifecycle at the virtual swap layer
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the management of swap entry lifecycle to the virtual swap layer, adding two fields to the swap descriptor: in_swapcache and swap count. It re-implements all swap entry lifecycle API functions in the virtual swap layer, simplifying the logic by not implementing swap count continuation for now.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/21] mm: swap: decouple virtual swap slot from backing store
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the virtual swap slot from its backing store, allowing a swap entry to be backed by one of four options: physical swap slot, zero swap page, compressed object in zswap pool, or in-memory page. This change enables the use of zswap and zero swap page optimization without reserving a swapfile slot, resulting in significant disk savings for hosts with high memory usage. The patch also introduces new requirements for batched swap operations to ensure consistency across entries with different backing states.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/21] zswap: do not start zswap shrinker if there is no physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the zswap shrinker from starting if there are no physical swap slots available, which is a consideration for virtualized swap environments where pre-allocated swap slots do not exist.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/21] swap: do not unnecesarily pin readahead swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes swap readahead by not unnecessarily pinning entries in the same virtual swap cluster as the target entry, which is already pinned by the caller. The patch introduces a new function `vswap_same_cluster` to check if two virtual swap entries belong to the same vswap cluster. It then uses this function to skip unnecessary pinning of readahead entries that are already protected by the target entry's cluster. This change aims to improve performance and reduce overhead in swap operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/21] swapfile: remove zeromap bitmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the zeromap bitmap from the swapfile code in the Linux kernel. The zeromap bitmap was used to track zero pages, but since zero swap entries are now treated as a separate backend in the virtual swap layer, this bitmap is no longer needed. Removing it saves memory overhead by eliminating 1 bit per swap page. This change does not affect behavior and only optimizes memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/21] memcg: swap: only charge physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) swap handling to only charge physical swap slots, rather than virtual swap slots. This change is made possible by the removal of zswap and zero-filled swap page optimization from consuming physical swap space. The patch updates various functions in the memcontrol code to record and clear swap charges for memcgs, ensuring that only actual physical swap usage is accounted for.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 19/21] swap: simplify swapoff using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies and optimizes the swapoff process by utilizing virtual swap slots stored at page table entries, eliminating the need for a page table walk. Instead of traversing all VMAs, it iterates through allocated swap slots on the swapfile, finds their corresponding virtual swap slots, and faults them in. This results in cleaner code and a performance improvement of about 13% kernel CPU time reduction compared to the original baseline.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 20/21] swapfile: replace the swap map with bitmaps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the swap map in the Linux kernel's swapfile code with two bitmaps, one for tracking allocated swap entries and another for bad swap entries. This change is made possible by moving the swap count state to the virtual swap layer, which reduces the number of states that need to be tracked from 4 to 3. The use of bitmaps instead of a single array saves 6 bits per swap entry, reducing memory usage. The patch updates the swap_info_struct structure in include/linux/swap.h and modifies several functions in mm/swapfile.c to work with the new bitmap-based representation.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the vswap_free function by batching contiguous free calls to reduce overhead on non-disk-swap backends. It achieves this by introducing a new release_vswap_slot_nr function that takes an additional 'nr' parameter, which specifies the number of slots to be released. This allows for more efficient handling of consecutive free operations and reduces unnecessary lock drops.
